common_name: Sikes Dwarf Oakleaf Hydrangea (Compact Oakleaf Hydrangea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Sikes Dwarf is the most compact of the commonly available oakleaf hydrangea cultivars — staying 3–4 feet tall, half the size of the standard species.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Sikes Dwarf is a compact oakleaf hydrangea (Hydrangea quercifolia) selected for its smaller, more manageable size — reaching 3–4 feet tall and wide — compared to the standard oakleaf hydrangea species that can grow 6–8 feet. Like the species, Sikes Dwarf has all the outstanding multi-season qualities that make oakleaf hydrangea the finest native flowering shrub for the Gulf South: conical white flower panicles from June through August that dry to tan-parchment and persist ornamentally through winter; large, distinctive oak-shaped leaves with outstanding reddish-purple to burgundy fall color; attractive cinnamon-orange peeling bark for winter interest; and excellent adaptation to the heat, humidity, clay soils, and partial to full shade of Gulf South gardens. Oakleaf hydrangea is native to the southeastern United States, making it perfectly adapted to zone 9 conditions without the special care that exotic hydrangea species may require. In deep shade (3 hours or less of sun), it flowers less but still provides outstanding foliage, bark, and fall color. The compact size makes Sikes Dwarf suitable for foundation plantings, shade borders, and garden positions where the full-size species would be too large.

common_name: Ruby Slippers Oakleaf Hydrangea (Dwarf Red-Turning Oakleaf)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; Ruby Slippers is a more compact selection than standard oakleaf hydrangea — staying 3–4 feet tall and wide — selected for its distinctive flower color change from white to deep rose-red as the flowers age.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Ruby Slippers is the most distinctive compact oakleaf hydrangea cultivar — selected not only for its reduced size (3–4 feet, compared to 6–8 feet for the species) but for its exceptional flower color change: the large, conical flower panicles open white in early summer and then turn progressively to deep rose-pink and finally rich ruby-red as they age through summer, providing an extended color display that ranges from pure white in June through rich rose-red by August. This color change from white to red is unique among oakleaf hydrangeas and gives Ruby Slippers a dramatically different appearance from the typical white-only oakleaf cultivars. Like all Hydrangea quercifolia cultivars, Ruby Slippers is a southeastern US native fully adapted to the heat, humidity, clay soils, and shade of the Gulf South — requiring no special care and developing excellent reddish-purple fall foliage color and cinnamon peeling bark for winter interest. The multi-season ornamental qualities, compact size, and distinctive red flower color make Ruby Slippers one of the most celebrated oakleaf hydrangea introductions of the early twenty-first century.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Mathotiana is a vigorous grower that benefits from planting in the fall to allow root establishment before summer heat arrives.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Mathotiana is one of the most enduring and widely grown Camellia japonica cultivars — a large-flowering formal double with rose-form to formal double, deep crimson to purplish-crimson flowers 4–5 inches across that are extraordinarily full and perfectly symmetrical. Mathotiana is sometimes called 'Purple Dawn' due to the purple-red to deep crimson color that distinguishes it from red camellia cultivars — the specific blue-red tone is unique and contributes to the flower's almost imperial visual richness. The flowers bloom in midseason (December through February in zone 9) on a vigorous upright to spreading plant that can ultimately grow 8–15 feet tall if unpruned. Mathotiana has been in cultivation since the early nineteenth century and is one of the foundational camellia cultivars — it has been in continuous cultivation longer than almost any other named japonica cultivar, reflecting its reliability, flower quality, and overall performance. In zone 9, Mathotiana performs as an exceptionally long-lived landscape shrub — camellia specimens of 50–100+ years are common in Southern historic gardens. Prune immediately after flowering (late winter/early spring) to control size and shape; avoid summer pruning that removes the following season's flower buds.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Kramer's Supreme is unusually tolerant of cold — one of the most cold-hardy red camellia cultivars available, surviving temperatures to 10–15°F.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Kramer's Supreme is one of the most significant Camellia japonica cultivars because it is reliably fragrant — a quality extremely rare in camellias, which are otherwise almost completely scentless. The large (4–5 inch), peony-form to informal double, vivid turkey-red flowers have a light but distinct, pleasant fragrance that is surprising in a camellia, making this cultivar uniquely valuable for gardens where fragrance is a priority. The flower color is an especially vivid, pure turkey-red without the blue-purple tones of Mathotiana — a clean, electric red that is among the most brilliant flower colors available for the winter garden. Kramer's Supreme blooms in midseason (November–February in zone 9), providing brilliant red winter color when few other shrubs are in bloom. Its exceptional cold hardiness (to zone 6, surviving temperatures to 10–15°F) makes it the most reliably winter-hardy red camellia cultivar, growing further north than most other japonica types. The upright to spreading habit grows 6–10 feet tall with regular pruning. In zone 9, Kramer's Supreme is an outstanding long-lived landscape shrub that requires only light pruning after bloom and supplemental iron (from acidic fertilizer) to prevent chlorosis in alkaline Gulf Coast soils.

planting.note: Plant container vines in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Blue Moon requires a very sturdy trellis, pergola, or arbor that can support the mature vine weight — wisteria vines become extremely heavy over time.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade on a sturdy support structure; Blue Moon is the most outstanding American wisteria cultivar available — a selection of Wisteria macrostachya (Kentucky wisteria, native to the central United States) that combines the non-invasive native wisteria's better behavior with several remarkable additional qualities: exceptional cold hardiness (to -40°F, reliably hardy to zone 3 — far hardier than any Asian wisteria), spectacular long racemes (8–12 inches) of silvery-blue-lavender fragrant flowers, and most remarkably, repeat blooming — producing a main spring flower flush in May followed by two or more additional bloom flushes in summer, a quality almost completely absent in single-blooming wisteria. The ability to bloom multiple times per season gives Blue Moon a greatly extended flowering season (May through August) compared to the single brief bloom of Asian wisteria. Like all W. macrostachya selections, Blue Moon is well-behaved — it does not spread invasively into natural areas as Asian wisterias do. In zone 9, it blooms most reliably with adequate chill hours (zone 9a); the floral display is exceptional in zones 3–8. Established vines bloom at a younger age than Asian wisteria and require less time to become established performers.
